fact: March 8 sits exactly 67 days into the year, which means there are still 298 days left to go!
In the Northern Hemisphere, March 8 falls smack in the middle of when maple trees start making their sweet, sticky sap that becomes maple syrup.
March 8 typically marks the peak of spring mating season for outdoor cats in many northern places, which is why cats seem extra active around this time.
The sun rises about 2 minutes earlier each day around March 8 in most northern places, so mornings get brighter super fast.
March 8 lands right in Pisces season, making it a day ruled by the dreamy, swimming fish of the zodiac.
In Australia and other southern places, March 8 is when many spiders start spinning their biggest, most impressive webs before winter hits.
Snow that falls around March 8 in cold places is often extra wet and heavy because the air temperature hovers right around freezing.
March 8 sits just 12 days before the spring equinox, when day and night become exactly equal length everywhere on Earth.
Earthworms typically become super active around this date, wiggling up to the surface in huge numbers as the soil starts to warm up in northern climates.
March 8 usually marks when many hibernating animals like groundhogs and bears start stirring in their dens, even if they don't come out yet.
In northern places, the sunset sky around March 8 often turns the most brilliant orange and pink colors because of how the sun hits the atmosphere at this angle.
